title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Back from the Dead: The Ethics of De-extinction

texts.A: The prospect of bringing back the woolly mammoth or the passenger pigeon is often presented as a triumph of genetic engineering. The hype surrounding these projects has captured the public imagination, but enthusiasm is not a substitute for rigorous ecological science, and the resources consumed could be far better spent elsewhere. [37]However, I believe this is a dangerous diversion of resources; every dollar spent on 'resurrecting' a single extinct species is a dollar not spent on protecting the thousands of living species currently on the brink of collapse.[/37] [39]Furthermore, an animal is more than its DNA; it is a product of its environment and social structures, both of which are now gone.[/39] We would be creating biological curiosities, not restoring ecosystems. Proponents speak of reviving entire ecological networks, but the complex web of interdependencies that once sustained these species has long since unravelled beyond any realistic possibility of reconstitution. The passion for resurrection is, at its heart, a refusal to accept the uncomfortable truth that some past mistakes cannot simply be engineered away.
texts.B: De-extinction is not about vanity; it is about ecological restoration. The simplistic notion that resurrected species would be ill-adapted misfits ignores the remarkable resilience and adaptability that defines life on Earth—species regularly colonize new habitats and thrive beyond their original range. Large herbivores like the mammoth played a crucial role in maintaining the grasslands of the Arctic. [38]I disagree with the view that this technology is a threat to current conservation; on the contrary, the advancements made in cloning and synthetic biology will provide new tools to save species that are struggling today.[/38] [40]That said, we must be careful not to view this as a 'get out of jail free' card for our past environmental sins.[/40] Resurrecting one species does not justify the destruction of another. Conservation biology has long been constrained by a purely defensive posture; de-extinction offers a rare opportunity to go on the offensive and genuinely restore lost biodiversity.
texts.C: The moral imperative to pursue de-extinction is clear: if we caused the extinction, we have a duty to reverse it. Critics often conflate two distinct scenarios—bringing back a species to a carefully restored habitat versus releasing it into a radically altered landscape—but they are not the same challenge. [37]I share the view that this field could revolutionize conservation, creating a 'bio-bank' that acts as an insurance policy for the planet's biodiversity.[/37] [39]I am less convinced than some, however, that these animals would be 'out of place'; many ecosystems have remained relatively stable and could easily re-integrate their former inhabitants.[/39] We should embrace the role of 'gardeners' of the Earth, using our technology to heal the scars we have left behind. This is not a romantic fantasy but a rigorous scientific endeavour with the potential to transform our relationship with the natural world from one of exploitation into genuine stewardship. The more pressing question is whether we have the ethical framework to do so responsibly and with genuine benefit to surviving ecosystems.
texts.D: The hype around de-extinction ignores the potential for catastrophic 'eco-shocks.' The scientific literature on invasive species is replete with examples of well-intentioned reintroductions that triggered cascading and irreversible ecological disasters. Introducing a resuscitated species into a modern ecosystem is essentially introducing an invasive species from the past. [38]I share the concern that we are prioritizing 'charismatic megafauna' over the less glamorous work of habitat protection.[/38] [40]Unlike those who believe we can control the outcome, I fear that we are playing with a complex system we don't fully understand.[/40] The ethics of creating a sentient being just to satisfy our scientific curiosity are deeply questionable. We should focus on the future, not a romanticized version of the past. Restoring existing degraded ecosystems through native planting, wetland rehabilitation, and reduced habitat fragmentation would yield demonstrably greater biodiversity gains than any headline-grabbing de-extinction project. We owe the natural world something more substantive than spectacle; we owe it the unglamorous, painstaking work of genuine ecosystem repair that produces lasting results rather than satisfying headlines.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the impact of de-extinction research on general conservation efforts?
questions.explanation: C believes de-extinction is a 'revolution' and an 'insurance policy' for biodiversity. A, however, sees it as a 'dangerous diversion of resources' that takes money away from protecting living species.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to A regarding the prioritization of de-extinction over other conservation work?
questions.explanation: A argues that resources are being diverted from 'living species.' D shares this concern, stating that we are 'prioritizing charismatic megafauna' over the 'less glamorous work of habitat protection.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's perspective on whether resurrected animals could successfully live in today's world?
questions.explanation: C believes ecosystems are 'stable' and could 'easily re-integrate' species. B shares this logic by arguing that resuscitating large herbivores is 'about ecological restoration' of specific areas like the Arctic.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on our ability to predict the consequences of reintroducing extinct species?
questions.explanation: B presents de-extinction as a tool for 'restoration' with specific ecological goals. D disagrees, fearing we are 'playing with a complex system we don't fully understand' and risking 'catastrophic eco-shocks.'
